prompt: Name one commonly-praised idea, trend, or practice in modern knowledge work that you think is overrated, and explain why.
response:
One idea that is often praised in modern knowledge work but can be overrated is the concept of multitasking or task-switching. Many people boast about their ability to juggle multiple tasks at once, believing it to be a sign of productivity and efficiency. However, research has consistently shown that trying to do more than one thing at a time can actually have detrimental effects on both the quality and quantity of your work.

Though it may feel like you're getting more done, your brain isn't wired to focus on two cognitively demanding tasks at the same time. When you try to multitask, your brain has to constantly switch between tasks, which requires a lot of cognitive effort. This process, known as context switching, can lead to information overload and decrease the overall quality of your work.

In fact, a study by the University of Michigan found that task-switching can temporarily reduce your IQ by as much as 10 points. Another study at the University of California Irvine found that stopping to check an email or Slack message, for example, can take an average of 23 minutes to refocus on the original task.

The alternative to multitasking is single-tasking or focused work. This means dedicating a set time to work on one task without interruption, also known as deep work. By focusing entirely on one project at a time, you can leverage your brain's natural ability to immerse itself in a task, leading to better results and higher productivity.

To practice this alternative, consider using a productivity method like Pomodoro or the Eisenhower Matrix, which both encourage single-tasking and prioritizing tasks based on importance and urgency. Blocking out time in your calendar for deep work, shutting off notifications, and establishing boundaries with colleagues can also help you minimize distractions and make better use of your time.

In summary, while multitasking may seem like an effective way to get more done, it often backfires in terms of quality and focus. Embracing single-tasking and deep work, on the other hand, can lead to higher-quality work, better time management, and reduced stress levels in the long run.
